prevalence.source: Gynecology epidemiology syntheses; adolescent health surveys
prevalence.trend: stable; underreported due to normalization of symptoms

incidence.value: Common at menarche and adolescence; recurrent monthly episodes

burden.dalyPerCapita: moderate QOL and productivity impact; low mortality

vulnerablePopulations: women with limited access to gynecology care
epidemiology.diagnosticCriteria: Clinical diagnosis for primary dysmenorrhea; evaluate for secondary causes (endometriosis, fibroids) when severe, progressive, or refractory

publicHealthSystem.estimatedPatients: Tens of millions of women affected
publicHealthSystem.controlRate: Moderate to high with NSAIDs and hormonal therapy

regionalSpecificities.diagnosisCriteria: Primary care and gynecology assessment; pelvic ultrasound for suspected secondary causes
regionalSpecificities.treatmentProtocol: NSAIDs, combined oral contraceptives or progestins, heat/exercise; referral for suspected endometriosis
regionalSpecificities.regionalChallenges: Delayed diagnosis of secondary causes and stigma around menstrual pain
regionalSpecificities.strongPoints: Effective low-cost treatments available in SUS and private care
